meta.title: CI/CD Pipelines
meta.description: CI/CD pipeline design with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, secret handling, and workflow patterns

items.text: Are workflow triggers scoped correctly (not running on every push)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Workflows should trigger on appropriate events: pull_request for checks, push to main for deploys. Use path filters to skip irrelevant runs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-broad triggers waste CI minutes, slow feedback, and create unnecessary noise. Running deploys on feature branches is dangerous.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check trigger events (on:). Verify path filters exclude docs, README changes. Ensure deploy workflows only run on main/release branches.
baseContent.exampleComment: This deploy workflow triggers on push to all branches. Restrict to push: branches: [main] to prevent accidental deployments.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope triggers to appropriate branches and paths. Use path filters to skip irrelevant changes.

items.text: Are job dependencies (needs:) configured correctly for parallel execution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Independent jobs should run in parallel. Dependent jobs should use needs: to declare dependencies. Use if: to control conditional execution.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sequential jobs when parallel is possible wastes time. Missing needs: can run deploy before tests pass.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that independent jobs (lint, test, build) run in parallel. Verify deploy jobs depend on all test/build jobs.
baseContent.exampleComment: Lint and test run sequentially but are independent. Remove the needs: dependency to run them in parallel.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run independent jobs in parallel. Only use needs: for genuine dependencies.

items.text: Are dependencies and build artifacts cached between runs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cache package manager downloads (npm, pip, maven), build outputs, and other slow-to-recreate artifacts between CI runs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without caching, every run downloads and installs all dependencies from scratch, adding 2-10 minutes per run.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for cache actions or built-in caching configuration. Verify cache keys include lockfile hashes for invalidation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This workflow installs dependencies from scratch every run. Add a cache step using the lockfile hash as the cache key.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cache dependencies using lockfile hashes. Cache build outputs when possible.

items.text: Are job timeouts configured to prevent runaway builds?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Jobs should have timeout-minutes set to prevent stuck builds from consuming resources indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A hung test or build can run for hours, consuming CI minutes and blocking other jobs. Default timeouts are often 6 hours.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for timeout-minutes on jobs. Set a reasonable limit based on expected run time (2-3x normal duration).
baseContent.exampleComment: This job has no timeout. If a test hangs, it runs for 6 hours. Add timeout-minutes: 15.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set timeouts on all jobs. Use 2-3x the expected duration as the limit.

items.text: Are pipeline steps idempotent (safe to re-run)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pipeline steps should be safe to re-run without side effects. Deploy steps should handle already-deployed states gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Retrying a failed pipeline should not create duplicates, send duplicate notifications, or corrupt state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check deploy and notification steps for idempotency. Verify re-running the pipeline won't cause double-deploys or duplicate records.
baseContent.exampleComment: This step creates a GitHub release but doesn't check if one already exists. Add a check to skip if the release exists.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make pipeline steps idempotent. Re-running should produce the same result as the first run.

items.text: Are all secrets stored in CI secret management (not hardcoded in pipeline files)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Passwords, tokens, and API keys must be stored in the CI platform's secret storage, never in workflow files or environment variables in plain text.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets in pipeline files are visible to everyone with repository access. They persist in git history even after deletion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search workflow files for hardcoded tokens, passwords, or API keys. Verify all sensitive values use ${{ secrets.* }}.
baseContent.exampleComment: The API key is hardcoded in the workflow file. Store it as a repository secret and reference it with ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Store all secrets in CI secret management. Never hardcode credentials in pipeline files.

items.text: Do CI tokens and service accounts have minimum necessary permissions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CI service accounts should only have permissions needed for their tasks. GitHub tokens should use minimal permissions: scope.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-privileged CI tokens can be used to modify code, delete branches, access other repos, or exfiltrate secrets.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check permissions: blocks in GitHub Actions. Verify service account roles in deployment pipelines are scoped to minimum.
baseContent.exampleComment: This workflow has permissions: write-all. Scope it to contents: read, pull-requests: write — only what's needed.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Apply least privilege to CI tokens. Scope permissions to the minimum needed for each job.

items.text: Are workflows triggered by PRs from forks handled securely?

baseContent.whatItMeans: pull_request_target runs with repository secrets but in the context of the target branch. Forked PRs with pull_request don't get secrets. Mixing these incorrectly is dangerous.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Giving secrets to forked PR code allows attackers to exfiltrate credentials by submitting malicious PRs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for pull_request_target with checkout of PR code. This combination gives secrets to untrusted code.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses pull_request_target with actions/checkout@PR. A malicious PR can steal secrets. Use pull_request instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never combine pull_request_target with checking out PR code. Use pull_request for untrusted code.

items.text: Are staging and production secrets separated (different values per environment)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Staging and production should use different credentials, API keys, and service accounts. Use environment protection rules.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shared credentials mean a staging breach compromises production. Environment separation limits blast radius.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the same secrets are used for staging and production. Verify production deployments use environment protection rules.
baseContent.exampleComment: The staging and production database use the same credentials. Create separate service accounts per environment.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Separate credentials per environment. Use environment protection rules for production deployments.

items.text: Are required status checks enforced on the main branch?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Branch protection should require CI checks to pass before merging. Tests, lint, and build must all succeed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without required checks, broken code can be merged to main, affecting all developers and potentially deploying bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check branch protection rules. Verify required status checks include tests, lint, and build. Ensure checks can't be bypassed.
baseContent.exampleComment: The main branch has no required status checks. Enable branch protection and require CI to pass before merging.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Require status checks on main. Never allow merging without passing CI.

items.text: Are tests stable (no flaky tests that are retried blindly)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should pass consistently. Automatic retry of failed tests masks flaky tests rather than fixing them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flaky tests erode trust in CI. Teams start ignoring failures, allowing real bugs to slip through.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for automatic retry configurations. Look at recent CI history for tests that intermittently fail.
baseContent.exampleComment: This job retries failed tests up to 3 times. Fix the flaky tests instead of retrying. Track flaky tests and fix them.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fix flaky tests, don't retry them. Track and prioritize flaky test fixes.

items.text: Are code coverage thresholds enforced without being gamed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Coverage thresholds should prevent regressions but not encourage writing meaningless tests just to hit a number.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Coverage without quality leads to tests that exercise code without asserting anything. Overly strict gates slow development.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check coverage configuration. Verify the threshold is reasonable (70-85%). Look for tests with no assertions.
baseContent.exampleComment: 100% coverage is required but many tests have no assertions. Lower the threshold and focus on meaningful test quality.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set reasonable coverage thresholds. Focus on test quality over quantity.

items.text: Is there a clear rollback strategy?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deployments should have a documented and tested rollback mechanism. One-click rollback to the previous version.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rollback, a bad deployment means either a forward-fix (slow) or manual intervention (error-prone).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the deployment process supports rollback. Verify it's been tested. Check for database migration rollback coordination.
baseContent.exampleComment: The deploy script has no rollback capability. Add a rollback job that deploys the previous image tag.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every deployment must have a rollback plan. Test rollbacks regularly.

items.text: Is progressive deployment used for production (canary, blue-green, feature flags)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production deployments should roll out progressively: canary to a small percentage, then gradually expand. Use feature flags for risky changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deploying to 100% immediately means bugs affect all users. Progressive deployment limits blast radius.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the deployment strategy supports canary or blue-green. Verify monitoring is in place to catch issues during rollout.
baseContent.exampleComment: This deploys directly to 100% of production. Consider a canary deployment that serves 5% of traffic first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deploy progressively to production. Monitor metrics during rollout and automate rollback on errors.

items.text: Do production deployments require manual approval?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production deployments should require explicit approval. Use environment protection rules or manual gates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Automatic production deploys on merge mean any merged code immediately reaches users. A bad merge causes an instant outage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if production deployments have approval gates. Verify the approval can't be bypassed.
baseContent.exampleComment: Production deploys automatically on merge to main. Add a manual approval step or environment protection rule.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Require explicit approval for production deployments. Automate staging deploys for testing.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Teams should be notified of deployment successes and failures via Slack, email, or other channels.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without notifications, failed deployments go unnoticed. Teams don't know when their code is live.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for notification steps in deploy workflows. Verify both success and failure cases send notifications.
baseContent.exampleComment: This deploy workflow has no failure notification. Add a Slack notification on failure so the team can respond quickly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Notify on deployment success and failure. Include deploy version and environment in notifications.

items.text: Are third-party actions pinned to specific versions or commit SHAs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Third-party GitHub Actions should be pinned to a commit SHA or specific version tag, not @latest or @main.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An unpinned action can be updated by its maintainer to include malicious code. SHA pinning prevents supply chain attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for actions using @main, @latest, or unpinned major versions. Verify they use SHA pins or specific version tags.
baseContent.exampleComment: uses: actions/checkout@main is unpinned. Pin to a SHA: uses: actions/checkout@abc123...
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pin third-party actions to commit SHAs. Use Dependabot to keep them updated.

items.text: Are runner images specified explicitly (not just 'latest')?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Specify runner OS versions (ubuntu-22.04 instead of ubuntu-latest) for reproducible builds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ubuntu-latest changes when GitHub updates the default. This can break builds when OS packages or tool versions change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check runs-on: for 'latest' tags. Pin to specific versions for reproducibility.
baseContent.exampleComment: runs-on: ubuntu-latest may change. Use ubuntu-22.04 for consistent builds.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pin runner images for reproducible builds. Accept 'latest' only if you want automatic updates.

items.text: Are workflow expressions protected against script injection?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Using ${{ github.event.issue.title }} directly in run: steps allows injection. Use environment variables instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A PR title or issue body containing shell commands will execute in the workflow context, potentially exfiltrating secrets.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for ${{ }} expressions inside run: blocks. These should be passed as environment variables instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: run: echo ${{ github.event.comment.body }} allows injection. Use env: COMMENT: ${{ github.event.comment.body }} and echo $COMMENT.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use ${{ }} directly in run: steps. Pass values through env: variables.
